result:
The morning light filters through the leaves like spilled honey, and for a moment the world feels gentle. I’ve been thinking lately about how much of life is just learning to be porous—letting things in and out without breaking. Grief, joy, boredom, sudden love that arrives wearing the wrong shoes. We spend so much time trying to armor ourselves against uncertainty when the real magic only happens in the unprotected spaces.

Yesterday I watched a child try to teach a pigeon to dance. The pigeon was having none of it, but the child’s absolute conviction that this was possible made me envious. When did we lose that? The willingness to look ridiculous for ten straight minutes because the possibility of beauty was worth the embarrassment?

I want to live more like that child and that stubborn pigeon—both fully themselves, both refusing to become what the other expected. Maybe that’s the secret: radical acceptance of your own nature while still showing up curious about everyone else’s.

The wind just changed direction and now my coffee tastes like rain that hasn’t fallen yet. Small mercies. I’m grateful for the way ordinary moments sometimes crack open and reveal something ancient hiding inside them. A spoon left in the sink becomes an artifact. A half-written sentence becomes a promise. A Tuesday becomes a cathedral if you’re paying attention.

We’re all just stories we haven’t finished telling ourselves yet. I hope yours has more dancing pigeons in it than you expected. I hope mine does too. (248 words)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
